article_title: n8n Webhooks Abused Since October 2025 to Deliver Malware via Phishing Emails
core_thesis: Threat actors have been exploiting n8n, a legitimate AI workflow automation platform, since October 2025 to conduct phishing campaigns that deliver malware and fingerprint devices. By leveraging n8n's trusted *.app.n8n.cloud subdomain and webhook functionality, attackers bypass traditional security filters, turning a productivity tool into a delivery vehicle for persistent remote access.
detailed_summary: Cisco Talos researchers discovered that attackers are abusing n8n's webhook feature to send phishing emails containing URLs that point to n8n-hosted webhooks. These URLs, which use the legitimate *.app.n8n.cloud subdomain, appear trustworthy and bypass security filters. When a victim clicks the link, it triggers an n8n workflow that returns an HTML page (e.g., a CAPTCHA) and then initiates a download of a malicious payload from an external host. The payload is often an executable or MSI installer that deploys modified versions of legitimate RMM tools like Datto or ITarian Endpoint Management, establishing persistence via C2 connection. Another abuse case involves embedding invisible tracking pixels hosted on n8n webhook URLs in emails; when the email is opened, the pixel sends an HTTP GET request with tracking parameters (e.g., victim's email) to fingerprint the device. The volume of such emails increased 686% from January 2025 to March 2026. The article emphasizes that the same automation features designed for productivity are being repurposed for malicious automation, and security teams must monitor low-code platforms to prevent abuse.

entities.name: Cisco Talos

entities.name: Sean Gallagher
entities.role: Cisco Talos researcher co-authoring the analysis.

entities.name: Omid Mirzaei
entities.role: Cisco Talos researcher co-authoring the analysis.

entities.name: n8n
entities.role: AI workflow automation platform whose webhook feature is being abused by threat actors.

entities.name: Datto
entities.role: Legitimate RMM tool modified by attackers to establish persistence.

entities.name: ITarian Endpoint Management
entities.role: Legitimate RMM tool modified by attackers to establish persistence.

key_data_points: Threat actors have been abusing n8n webhooks since October 2025 to deliver malware via phishing emails (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates how trusted SaaS platforms can be weaponized for initial access and C2).

n8n webhook URLs use the legitimate subdomain *.app.n8n.cloud, making them appear trustworthy and bypassing traditional security filters (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this technique can be used to evade email security and deliver payloads).

One campaign embeds an n8n webhook link in an email claiming to be a shared document; clicking triggers a CAPTCHA, then downloads a malicious payload from an external host (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: provides a blueprint for social engineering with CAPTCHA to evade automated analysis).

The payload is an executable or MSI installer that deploys modified versions of legitimate RMM tools (Datto, ITarian) to establish persistence via C2 connection (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: shows how to use legitimate tools for persistence, blending in with normal admin activity).

Another abuse case uses invisible tracking pixels hosted on n8n webhook URLs to fingerprint victims by sending HTTP GET requests with tracking parameters like email address (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: useful for reconnaissance and targeting).
Volume of emails containing n8n webhook URLs increased 686% from January 2025 to March 2026 (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: indicates growing adoption of this technique by threat actors).
conclusions: n8n's webhook feature, designed for legitimate automation, is being repurposed by attackers to deliver malware and fingerprint devices, leveraging the platform's trusted domain to bypass security filters. This highlights the dual-use risk of low-code automation platforms.

The attack chain involves embedding an n8n webhook URL in a phishing email, which when clicked triggers a workflow that returns a CAPTCHA and then downloads a malicious payload from an external host. The payload uses legitimate RMM tools for persistence, making detection harder.

Security teams should monitor for unexpected n8n webhook URLs in email traffic and consider blocking or inspecting traffic to *.app.n8n.cloud subdomains if not used legitimately. The article underscores the need to treat low-code platforms as potential attack vectors.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming interests as it details a novel technique for initial access and persistence using a legitimate SaaS platform (n8n). You can incorporate this into phishing simulations by setting up your own n8n webhook to deliver payloads or fingerprint targets, bypassing email security filters. The use of legitimate RMM tools for persistence is also a valuable tradecraft to blend in with normal admin activity. As a programmer, you can automate the entire workflow using n8n's API, making it a scalable attack vector. Additionally, the tracking pixel technique is useful for reconnaissance in targeted campaigns.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a free n8n cloud account and experimenting with webhook workflows to replicate the attack chain in a controlled lab environment. This will give you hands-on experience with the technique and help you develop detection rules. Also, explore how to chain multiple n8n workflows to automate post-exploitation tasks, such as data exfiltration or lateral movement, using the same trusted domain.
